indicators.name: Cumulative Treaty-Rights Infringement Risk — NE BC (Treaty 8)
indicators.summary: Post-Yahey precedent on cumulative effects creates elevated risk for further Treaty 8 corridor development.
indicators.note: Yahey v. BC (2021) found cumulative industrial development infringed Blueberry River First Nations' Treaty 8 rights. The decision applies in principle to NE BC corridor projects.

indicators.name: Crown Consultation Adequacy — Federal Major Projects
indicators.summary: After 2018 FCA Tsleil-Waututh ruling, federal projects face heightened procedural-adequacy expectations.
indicators.note: Post-2018 ruling raised the procedural bar; second-round consultation processes have become the working norm for federally-led major projects.

indicators.name: Hereditary vs. Elected Authority — Consent Risk
indicators.summary: Unresolved doctrine on which authority governs project consent where Aboriginal title is asserted but unproven.
indicators.note: Delgamuukw recognizes hereditary governance evidence. Tsilhqot'in confirms title-holders' decision-making rights. Practical application to specific projects varies.

indicators.name: Public-Record Evidence Strength — Major Projects
indicators.summary: Quality of publicly-available evidence varies by project; financing terms are often the weakest area.
indicators.note: Court records, EA filings, and regulatory submissions form a strong public evidence base. Internal financing terms, impact-benefit agreement contents, and equity-option specifics are typically not public.

indicators.name: Peace River Watershed Stress
indicators.summary: Cumulative flow alteration from existing and planned hydro + industrial withdrawals.
indicators.note: Reservoir conversion at Site C and upstream W.A.C. Bennett operations affect flow regime, sediment, and downstream ecosystem services.

indicators.name: Prairie Drought Conditions (multi-year)
indicators.summary: Multi-year precipitation deficit and snowpack decline pattern affects water-licence availability.
indicators.note: Affects industrial water-licence renewals and Indigenous water-rights claims in the Prairies; verify against current provincial drought-bulletin data before acting.

indicators.name: Industrial Water Demand — Pacific LNG Corridor
indicators.summary: LNG liquefaction is electricity-intensive but moderate direct water user; cooling water for power generation is the larger systemic factor.
indicators.note: Direct facility water demand is relatively modest; the bigger water question is upstream electricity generation if grid expansion drives new hydro or thermal generation.

indicators.name: First Nation Water-Rights Recognition Progress
indicators.summary: Domestic water rights are recognized in principle; allocation in practice still favours licensed industrial users.
indicators.note: Court recognition under Section 35 has strengthened; implementation through provincial water-licence systems lags. Watch C-61 federal First Nations Clean Water Act and related provincial reforms.

indicators.name: BC Grid — Transmission Constraints Northeast
indicators.summary: Industrial load growth in NE BC outpaces transmission build; BC Hydro has signalled multi-year reinforcement plans.
indicators.note: Affects timing for electrification-dependent projects including LNG, mining, and data centres.

indicators.name: AI / Data Centre Load Growth — Canadian Grids
indicators.summary: Hyperscaler data centre demand is a top driver of new load forecasts, with material implications for grid planning and siting.
indicators.note: Hyperscalers are siting based on power availability and cost. Indigenous-owned generation and grid-adjacent land becomes strategically relevant. Verify specific load forecasts in each utility's IRP.

indicators.name: Oil & Gas Infrastructure Exposure — Asset Stranding Risk
indicators.summary: Long-life pipeline and processing assets carry decade-scale recovery horizons; demand-trajectory uncertainty affects residual value.
indicators.note: Indigenous equity in oil & gas assets shares this exposure; pricing of asset acquisitions should reflect realistic demand-trajectory scenarios, not historical baselines.

indicators.name: Project Certainty — Composite Reading (Major Canadian Projects)
indicators.summary: Composite of consent, treaty, water, regulatory, and finance signals across active major-project pipeline.
indicators.note: This is a qualitative composite, not a market index. The trend reflects rising importance of Indigenous validation and water/grid realism, not project quality.

indicators.name: Indigenous Equity Participation — Major Projects
indicators.summary: Indigenous equity tranches are increasingly offered; community uptake and terms vary widely.
indicators.note: Cedar LNG (Indigenous majority) and CGL (10% option) illustrate range. Loan guarantees, cash-flow waterfall, and governance rights are the key terms to evaluate.

indicators.name: CIB Indigenous Equity Initiative — Utilisation Pattern
indicators.summary: Program has financed Indigenous equity in several major projects; specific terms vary by transaction.
indicators.note: CIB structure is debt secured against acquired equity and project cash flow. Specific interest rates, tenors, and recourse terms are transaction-specific.

indicators.name: Indigenous Debt Exposure — Cash-Flow-Backed Loans
indicators.summary: Where Indigenous equity is debt-financed against project cash flow, underperformance translates directly to community exposure.
indicators.note: The structure looks like 'free' equity if the project performs at base case; underperformance scenarios are where the carry shifts to the community.

indicators.name: FNFA Capital-Markets Access
indicators.summary: FNFA pooled-borrowing program has investment-grade ratings; spreads remain wider than equivalent municipal benchmarks.
indicators.note: Verify current ratings and spreads in each new bond issue prospectus.

indicators.name: Community Validation Gaps — Major Projects
indicators.summary: Public-record evidence rarely captures internal community ratification or dissent processes.
indicators.note: External assessments should not over-claim 'community consent' from band-council signatures alone. Check for membership votes, hereditary positions, and youth/elder council positions where public.

indicators.name: UNDRIP Implementation — Domestic Legal Effect
indicators.summary: Implementation pathway is established federally and in BC; specific litigation tests on consent standards are still developing.
indicators.note: Bill C-15 and BC DRIPA set the framework; courts are still working out the practical effect on individual project decisions.
